description: Subadult, probably a juvenile. Seen in flight from above, the bird had a medium-gray mantle with a striking, thick, black "M"-pattern. The inner primaries and outer secondaries (under the "humps" of the "M") were extensively white. The bird had a dusky collar and a dusky patch behind the eye. Otherwise, it was white. In this regard, I note that the tail tip was either entirely white or almost entirely white; wasn't positive.  Seen in direct comparison with an adult Bonaparte's Gull (sitting side-by-side on the water) the bird was notably larger.  In flight, its wings seemed notably long and narrow. Its insubstantial black legs were briefly noted on several (relatively) close passes.Behaviors: The bird was first seen in flight, passing quickly by from left to right at a distance of perhaps 250 feet. That is the closest we got to the bird. It was observed in flight--at all angles--for a full 2 minutes, at distances ranging from 250 feet to well over 1,000 feet. The bird flew about quickly and dartingly ("bat out of hell" comes to mind) most of the time it was under observation. It alighted on the water briefly (see above), then put into flight again.Call: Not heardPlumage: Juvenile? (see below)
